displayTitle: 2026-03-25 - Governance and Administration Committee - Petition of David Glen on behalf of Cornwall Park Leaseholders Association: Mandatory compensation to lessees for the value of their improvements
topic: Petition of David Glen on behalf of Cornwall Park Leaseholders Association: Mandatory compensation to lessees for the value of their improvements
description: Wednesday 25 March 2026 | Governance and Administration Committee Petition of David Glen on behalf of Cornwall Park Leaseholders Association: Mandatory compensation to lessees for the value of their improvements Cornwall Park Leaseholders Association Dr Peter Cameron, (Chairperson) Tanja Blinkhorn Don Tilbrook Gautam Jindal Cornwall Park Trust Board Adrienne Young-Cooper, Chair Cornwall Park Trust Board Murray Reade, Chief Executive Cornwall Park Trust Martina Llewellyn, Commercial Manager Cornwall Park Trust John Hannan, Legal Counsel
descriptionSummary: Petition of David Glen on behalf of Cornwall Park Leaseholders Association: Mandatory compensation to lessees for the value of their improvements. Cornwall Park Leaseholders Association. Dr Peter Cameron, (Chairperson)

chunks.text: Good morning. Good morning. You got anyone else? Shall I introduce myself? Yes, I'm Peter Cameron. Yeah. So another person. Great. Mm-mm. I'll just see if there's anyone else to set up. That's it. Right. Look, good morning. Welcome to the Governance and Administration Select Committee. We're here to talk about the petition of David Glenn on behalf of the Cornwall Park Leaseholders Association, mandatory compensation to leasees for the value of their improvements.

So we have 15 minutes with you as the Cornwall Park Leaseholders Association, and that will be followed by 15 minutes with the Cornwall Park Trust Board. It's the time, the 15 minutes is yours, but we would love it if you left some time at the end for us to be able to ask some questions. That would be much appreciated. Thank you, and over to you.

Tena koutou, talofa. Greetings, and thank you to the Select Committee for hearing our submission. Going to talk fast. My name is Peter Cameron. I work as a South Auckland GP. I've lived with my family in a Cornwall Park leasehold property since 2000. I'm acting chairperson of the Cornwall Park Leaseholder Association. And with me are other members of the committee, Gautham Jindal, Tania Blinkhorn, and also Mr.

Don Tillbrook, an expert in the history of the Cornwall Park leases. And we represent approximately 70 leaseholders out of the approximate 87 remaining. What we want is your help to find a solution in a difficult situation. It is possible to create a win-win for both leaseholders and the Cornwall Park Trust.

As we will show you, the new leases are unaffordable to the extent that virtually every leaseholder will have to walk away from their homes. We want leaseholders to be able to stay in their properties, and we believe this will work best for the Cornwall Park Trust's finances to support their excellent work to maintain the park, which is a national taonga.

What we request from the Select Committee is that you act on an established precedent and recommend to Parliament a re-examination of the Cornwall Park leases situation along the lines of the Lusk report. I'll explain soon what we mean by that. So I'll talk for a few minutes and leave, hopefully, plenty of time for questions. We'll explain why virtually no leaseholder can possibly renew their lease, even the proposed 3%, 4%, 5% version, and why the situation benefits neither the Cornwall Park Trust board nor the leaseholders, and how

you, as the subcommittee, can assist to put the situation back on track by doing the same thing as your predecessors did in the 1980s. This is an expensive document, the Park Board's submission. Looking to have input by legal teams and a PR machine, and it has many cherry-picked facts.

And, I just request, please don't let it sidetrack you from your understanding of what's essentially a really simple situation, which is that approximately 80 leasehold properties are yet to come up for lease renewal in the next five to seven years, and 59 of these properties are coming up for renewal by the end of 2030.

The leases are called Glasgow leases. They're structured so that the annual lease cost is fixed at 5% of the land value. This is paid then annually over 21 years. The problem is the land value. This determines the annual cost of the lease. Whetuangia Road represents the middle of the range of the value of properties in the trust portfolio.

So this is a graph showing the property value of houses in Whetuangia Road that are equivalent to the Park Board's properties, many of which are included. They are larger sites. As you can see, in the '20s, when the leases started, the value of the properties was about 2,400.

By 1980, 80,000. Coming up to 2020, two to 3 million. 2025, three to 5.5 million. I hope you can see that. And that is really the problem in a nutshell. Because the value of the property has gone up so much over the last couple of decades, a 5% lease at present would cost a leaseholder 150 to 275,000 per year.

This would be the new annual lease cost for families whose average family income is around 150,000 a year. The park's proposed a 3%, 4%, 5% offer you might have read about. But even with that, the first seven years, a leaseholder is looking at 90,000 Ks per year, and then it rises to finish at 150 the last seven years. So I'm sure if you put yourself in these people's situations, it's not surprising that every leaseholder has to leave their property.

Furthermore, the leases are not just unaffordable to the leaseholder, but they're unaffordable to anyone else. No one will buy a leased property with a scale of annual cost. The properties are practically unsaleable.Virtually all of the houses that have been sold have been sold back to the Cornwall Park Trust or one of its subsidiary companies for very low figures.

Cornwall Park Trust was recently buying these properties at 185K, which they call the backstop offer. This is more than the market would pay due to the unaffordable lease, but with few exceptions, much less than the leaseholder originally paid for the property, which they've subsequently maintained or improved.

Once the Cornwall Park Trust has the properties back, it can't lease them. They can now only rent them out, where they earn less and pay much more in expenses than they would earn with a reasonable lease. So this situation is exactly the same as when Parliament previously took action.

In Epsom in the 1980s, big trusts like the Dilworth Trust and the St John's Anglican Trusts were turning over their Glasgow's leases. There's hardly any Glasgow leases left anymore. At a time when land prices had increased astronomically, similar to the graph I demonstrated.

Large numbers of leaseholders were unable to afford the 5% leases and were having to leave their homes with minimal ability to sell. Parliament took notice and the impact it was having on so many people, so the Minister of Justice commissioned the Lusk Report. The Lusk Report went on to recommend various solutions, including the legal provision for compensation for value of improvements when people had to leave their properties under these circumstances.

And the results of these recommendations were very satisfactory. No law needed to be passed because the Dilworth and St John's Trust voluntarily changed their systems, in most case by offering a right of freehold, and this worked extremely well for their finances going forward.

Members of the select committee, please be aware of the real human cost to these families whose leases are still coming up for renewal in the next three to five years. Over 80 households means that hundreds of people are affected. Years of worrying as to how they can possibly afford the new lease, years of daily concern about their virtual loss of equity, which means they may never be able to buy a property again.

The Leaseholders Committee knows of cases of bankruptcy, of insolvency, of people going to live in tiny homes on their children's properties, of couples approaching 80 years old having to keep working to pay their lease. And we also know of a suicide that was said to have been triggered by the stress of this situation. We can see no sense or benefit to the Cornwall Park Trust in continuing in this direction.

Why are they not taking action? There seem to be only two possible reasons. One is that they don't believe that people are going to have to walk away from their properties, and the second is that they're taking advantage of the situation to acquire these properties at very cheap rates, and if so, this is unconscionable and inequitable.

We sincerely hope the Cornwall Park Trust doesn't continue to make the same mistake it did with the Auckland Showgrounds lease, where they refused to believe the lessee who said repeatedly that what was proposed was unaffordable. And the board were intransigent, the lessee walked away. This was a very expensive mistake for the trust board and reputationally damaging for its members, and was devastating for several businesses who were dependent on the Showgrounds.

So in summary, the leases are unaffordable. There is a parliamentary pre-precedent from the '80s and early '90s for taking action, and so we request that the select committee recommend to Parliament a formal review of the lease situation along the lines of the Lusk Report.

So, we're open to questions. Peter, could I ask-- Thank you very much for that, and I do have some questions. But just when you held up that graph at our end, the screen went a bit fuzzy, but it looks like it's fine now. So could you hold that graph up again, please? Yes, that's perfect. Thank you.

Yeah. Just so we can see that- So the-- Yeah, the orange is the average house price in Auckland, and the blue is the prices in Whareroa Road. And it's a sort of an example of the catch up of value of as property, so the cost of property rises as, I call it Epsomization, as the expensive properties ex-- Suburbs expand.

That's great. Yeah. Thanks, Peter. I really appreciate that. Just it did go a bit fuzzy at that point. So I've got a question from Andy Foster, and he-- I'll hand over to him for that question. Peter, thank you. Just wanted to find out what the situ-- So there's obviously a big change there in the rate at which the value has gone up for your properties vis-à-vis other properties in the area. Is there anything particular in terms of the nature of the zoning, any restrictions on the use of the land?

Is it subject to any parks management plan? Anything of that nature. And how is, to your knowledge, how is it being valued by the trust board? So those are very big questions with a lot of history around them. In principle, there's no difference to other sort of quarter acre properties. Since the land was subdivided by Logan Campbell, it's essentially in quarter acre lots.

The lease has restricted the ability to further subdivide the lots. And so the value of the property is actually equivalent to unimproved bare land in the eastern suburbs. This is probably a distraction, but there's been a long history of the leaseholder society also negotiating with the park board that it's not really fair to assess the value of the land from which 5% is

taken as unimproved land value in the eastern suburbs. But that's- Yeah. That's what it is, yeah. Where I was trying to go, Peter, is are they valuing it on, essentially what it sounds like is they're valuing it on what's usually called the highest and best use, assuming the ability to subdivide. Assuming the ability to develop intensively.

My question is whether that's actually permitted by the zoning, well, it clearly doesn't sound as though it's permitted by your lease conditions because they don't let you do it. And whether it's also subject to anything like a parks management plan, which says, "Look, we don't want to have high rise buildings," whatever it might be, around the outskirts of Cornwall Park.

So Don, are you able to comment on that? So my- Yes. Thanks, Don. This has been the subject of quite a lot of court action, actually. And the thinking was, well, how can you charge the rent on the highest and best use, and at the same time restrict that use?

And there was a dissenting opinion by the Chief Justice, but the other justices in the Supreme Court allowed it through. So, it is assessed on the highest and best use, and we are restricted in what we can do with it. Okay. That's helpful. Thank you.

Thank you. I've got a question here from Tom Rutherford. Thank you very much. I want to come to point nine in the petition, which says hardworking Kiwi families who have called these leasehold properties their home for several decades will be forced out of their homes and will be inadequately compensated for the capital improvements. I'm slightly playing devil's advocate here.

Can you tell me, when you make, or when the property owners make the capital improvements, did they know at the time that they lived on leasehold land? Yes. Yes, they did. So what's happened with the acceleration in value, is that the previous 80 years of historical precedent has changed. So it used to be said that, yes, of course, you're taking a risk in buying leasehold land, but from cycle to cycle of the leases renewing every 21 years, the properties tended to sell for about 50% of freehold value. And because the leases, the land was going up

in more of a linear way, and the lease costs were more predictable, the leases were affordable. And so, for example, with my place, I've perhaps stupidly done quite a lot of renovation work. It's a beautiful place that backs onto the park. And most leasehold properties were similar.

You can drive down, for example, Mangakiekie Ave, where I live, and you can see the leasehold properties generally as well maintained as the freehold ones. But the ones that are reverted to rental are starting to have a completely different look.

I guess my line of questioning is around, I can sympathize with the situation at hand. Yes, I understand. But when you- It's okay... purchase in a situation like this, and you have leasehold land, this is the element of risk that you take on by doing so, versus when you purchase a property that is freehold, for example.

And so I'm just-- It's not like this was not known at the time of purchase. Yes, the circumstances may have changed around how they enforce the leases, but it's not like that it was leasehold land has suddenly appeared out of nowhere, and that was a risk that the purchasers took on at the time of purchasing the property.

Yeah, that's a very good point. And, that is how anyone sensible would think. The issue is with the Glasgow lease, we've taken on a Glasgow lease, a lease structure which has now died out all over the world, where the value of the lease payable is absolutely fixed to the value of the land. The Cornwall Park, we want to talk with them about changing the lease.

They've come up with a couple of options which are almost as expensive. The value of the lease has not got any kind of commercial reality like a normal lease, which involves negotiation and acceptance. So we've got people who are in their homes, and all of a sudden, they're being asked for 150,000, 200,000 a year.Now, this situation, your predecessors did find unpalatable, and we're asking you to make a simple recommendation that this is not a situation just of commercial agreement and

Parliament interfering with a commercial contract. It's really a situation of an archaic lease format that in the past when this has come up, the lessor, in other words, the trusts, have seen that and have taken action. We want-... we don't want everyone-- Yeah.

Thank you. We have come to the end of your time now. I did let that run a little bit longer because those questions were important. But we're now going to move to the Cornwall Park Trust Board. So thank you for your input there, and we will now move to the other side of this equation. Thank you.
Thank you. Thank you.

We don't have the Cornwall Park Trust Board- Oh... with us. We don't seem to have them with us. So we may have to reschedule them. Sorry. They're outside. They're here. No, they are here. They're outside waiting for us. Do we say Thank you, Brinda.

We should- That was a face I wasn't expecting to see. I just was listening and I was like, "What?" She can stay on the call though, Madam Chair. So you can- She doesn't have the camera. Oh. No. No. So, yeah. They'll sort that. Yeah. It's fine.
Just listening. But somebody else is back. Mm-hmm.

So welcome. Thank you. Did you see that? Yes. We-- Yes, we- Yeah... on our phones. Right. Oh, yeah. Heard everything. You could have actually come in, so I'm sorry about that. No, we're all ears. We didn't realize you were sitting outside. You would've been welcome in here. Thank you. Thank you, and it's good that you did see it. So we have 15 minutes with you now, and I shall hand over to you for your presentation.
Thank you. And,

My name is Adrian Young-Cooper, and I'm the chair of the Cornwall Park Trust Board. With me is Murray Reid, CEO of the Cornwall Park Trust Board, and Martina Llewellyn, commercial manager who administers the leasehold, and John Hannon, behind me, legal counsel who's advised the Trust Board for almost 20 years.

Cornwall Park is a much-loved, heavily used park that includes a sheep and beef farm in the middle of Auckland. Everyone who uses the park will agree it's a true jewel in the crown among Auckland parks. It is uniquely beautiful and elegant and a large city park.

It provides a large island of tranquility for a wide range of forms of recreation, sports grounds, picnics, venues for weddings, running, walking, cycling, and a cafe, a restaurant, and an ice cream parlor. It provides a haven for both native and exotic flora and is an inland island for bird life.

Uniquely, it is also a working farm where families and other users can access a rural experience in the middle of the city. The park was the gift in 1901 of Sir John Logan Campbell, 125 years ago, who arrived in Tāmaki Makaurau as a ship's surgeon from Scotland via Sydney. He saw the light and came to New Zealand, in 1840.

His vision was a park of this nature. He also provided in 1908 a land endowment to his trustees to fund the development and operation of the park. The income from the endowment lands providing a fringe around the park. From the establishment of the park, these endowment properties have been leased to provide income to develop and maintain the park.

The park operates no cost to local and central government. The petitioners submit from the perspective of their personal wishes and a desire to improve their personal financial position, a position they entered into freely on the market and with full knowledge. Essentially, they want the ability to sell their leasehold interest for a price higher than the market presently bears.

Their public agitation may, in our view, have damaged the market's perception of these perpetual leaseholds. We, the trustees of the board, have legal fiduciary duties to the deed of trust established in 1901 and statutory trustee duties under the laws of New Zealand to preserve and enhance the assets and income of the trust, both the parkland and the endowment land.

The economics of perpetually renewable leases with fixed rent for 21 years, which is then reset each 21 years based on land value, are affected by general inflation, the property price cycle, and interest rates. This is, and always was, inherent in the nature of these leases.

Land subject to such leases is recognized as a sensible and conservative capital investment for trust boards. Most of the leasehold properties front onto or are closely adjacent to Cornwall Park.From a leaseholder's perspective, purchasing a perpetually renewable leasehold is a less costly way of gaining secure tenure of a property in a highly desirable area than buying freehold.

In some periods, inflation has significantly eroded the value of the rent income to the trust board over particularly the last parts of the 21-year lease term. In such periods, ground leases can be a very good deal for the lessee compared with the cost of borrowing to purchase an equivalent freehold property.

In other periods, property price inflation is or has been such that the rent increases sharply at the 21-year rent reset. We recognize that this may cause hardship to some lessees depending on their circumstances. We have regularly made, and continue to make, significant concessions where lessees do suffer hardship.

At the same time, we must adhere to our fiduciary duty to preserve the assets and income of the trust. We have progressively taken steps to modernize and modify the lease terms to assist lessees. In 2021, we specifically offered a substantial discount on the normal ground rent of 5% of land value to 3% for the first seven years, 4% for the next seven years, reverting to 5% for the last seven years.

We offered a backstop purchase to the lessee, of the lessee interest, to 66 owner-occupier leaseholds if they chose not to renew their leases. 30 lessees have taken up the offer or are in the process of doing so. Other steps are explained in the written submissions.

It's not correct that leasehold interests do not sell. Our written submission contains a table outlining sales of leases over the past 10 years. But from the petitioner's perspective, the prices realized may not meet their expectations. These are open market transactions and reflect how the real estate market in Auckland values the lessee interest being transacted.

What petitioners request Parliament to do would be fundamentally unconstitutional confiscation of private assets. We have provided two opinions to that effect from Sir Geoffrey Palmer, New Zealand's most eminent constitutional lawyer, in our written submissions. Sir Geoffrey Palmer said it would be particularly offensive for Parliament to legislate to deal with the situation of an individual trust board. The petitioners seem to ask for legislation to deal with the perpetually renewable ground leases generally.

This would affect many lessors across not just Auckland, but throughout New Zealand. We provide some details of the likely widespread effect in our written submissions. Comparisons with UK legislation dealing with leasehold property are not appropriate. The UK legislation deals with a different set of problems.

This is explained in our written submissions. We continue to engage in dialogue with leaseholders and the leaseholders' association. We have offered changes to lease structures. We have provided concessions to assist lessees who may have difficulties. We will continue to be open to changes that do not breach our fiduciary duties as trustees of Cornwall Park.

But it would be in breach of our legal fiduciary duties, and fundamentally unfair, for us to make the changes that the leaseholders request. In closing, I again reference Sir Geoffrey Palmer. It would also be constitutionally obnoxious for Parliament to legislate to adjust private contracts to force the changes the petitioners request on all lessors of ground leases. And just one comment. I don't want to go into all the comments that the petitioners made, but they made some comments about the showgrounds, and as we're not looking at the showgrounds today, I refute all those

statements, and the facts were not correct as they were described to you. Thank you. And thank you very much for your presentation. I do have some questions, and the first one comes from Andy Foster. Look, thank you, and unfortunately, we don't have your original written submission in front of us. So apparently it was given to us some weeks ago, and then I don't think any of us have actually got it.

So we might have to go and refer back to that. But the- We will. We'll make sure that we put... Yeah. Yes. We will do that. Can I just check? So we've heard that leases are likely to go to levels sort of 90, 100, 150, even $200,000 per property. What are you seeing the likelihood is when they're revalued, which is in the next few years? And then once it's got to that value, if you said 90,000, is that it's the 3%, and then it will be 120,000, then it'll be 150,000.

Are those roughly the kind of numbers that you're seeing? It does depend entirely on the value of the land at the time. Can you give us a range of what you're actually seeing? So, what are our most recent valuations, Martina? Our recent valuations have been starting at 60,000. But that also depends on the size of the land value.

Yep. No, I understand. Or the size of the land of the plot. Yep. And then there are the discounted rent, which is the 3%, 4%, and 5%. That is for eligible leases up until December 2028. They were eligible for that So you start at 60 and go up to- Then it's discounted for 3% of that.

So the actual land value is, when the value is established, it's 5%. Then we provide an internal discount to eligible leases. Yes. And then it's discounted to went down to 45,000 the first seven years, and then it went up that much. Okay. So your range is from 60. Forget the discount bit for the moment.

Mm-hmm. Your range is 60 to what? Well, it- Would be, roughly... it depends on the, just to be... The value of the land is set by two valuers. Yes. And if they can't agree, then there's an, they appoint an umpire. So- Yeah. You heard the question about highest and best use?

Yeah. So, I should just clarify that yes, that is the law that was tested all out. But how much is the top limit? It's a really- Oh, okay. It's going to be- So, it is a formula that relates- No, how much is it? How much. What's the dollar value? We do not know because it depends. Yeah. Mangakiekie Avenue property is valued regularly.

What are you getting today for it? But we- You must know what you're getting today. Okay, so just- Right. Sorry. The, it is always the outcome of the valuation process. The most recent valuation of a single family home property was- Was in Campbell Road.

And how much was it? That was at 60,000. No, how much was the actual valuation that was agreed, the unimproved value? I think I'd have to get back to you. Could you get back to us with that? Because it would be really good. Yeah. I think it'll be good to, it would be good to get a bit of a picture. Sure. Okay. So there's the other part. Sure. Yeah. Just that whole picture. Yes, sure. We, yes, we will provide the committee with- Yeah. That would be wonderful. Thank you... the last three or four valuations.

And the expect, and expected ones. Yeah. What the unimproved land value. That would be very helpful. Yeah. I think it's also the expected ones, because the whole thing is the change that's happened. The clarifications of- Oh, clarifications. Yeah. I think what we're looking for is not just the land value. But we're actually wanting to know how much leases these tenants have actually paid per those- Sure... properties.

Sure. We can- So you claimed at 200,000. Well, that's speculation about a future value. Mm. Which has not yet been determined. That's all I'm saying, is that it's a, there is a process. We can tell you the actual valuations that have been completed in the last five years.

That would be helpful. That would be helpful, yeah. And then what the actual ground rent is. I've got a question from Tom, but as this is- Just another quick question. Can we also... Look, because you're talking about the land value. Mm-hmm. But they're- I understand. These people have purchased a leasehold. Land that actually has property. Mm-hmm. And they're not actually... It's a leasehold property that they actually own.

Yes. And they're actually not able to develop the land. So, they're looking at their homes that they can't even renovate, and they have actually put improvements on the property, which is actually leasehold. Technically, most people, when they buy a leasehold, they don't actually put a lot of money into it. Correct. But, it's not something that you've actually taken care of.

These tenants have done that. So what we're actually wanting to know is what's the value of the land, because potentially those people who live around Cornwall Park, they have massive properties with lots of land, but their properties are not necessarily that big either. So we'd like to know what the valuation of the houses are without the improvements, if you have those. Mm-hmm. You would definitely have those data.

Yes, we do. Mm-hmm. So, what are those property values plus the land value, and you are actually charging lease, are based on the land value. So we just want to get facts- Yes... together so we can actually assess what's going on. Sure. That'd be great. Thank you. Um, Andy- Can I just finish? Yeah. Can I just...

The other part that they said is, look, if, they've said that a number of people, and it'll be good to know how many, have walked away from leases. What then happens with those properties? What do you do, and what's the revenue stream that you get from those, vis-à-vis the revenue stream that you get from leasing them out? Okay. So, the first thing, it's, if we just look at the recent past, we have had a look at how many, at what has happened with- Yeah... with properties in the last 10 years.

And so I think- Thank you. From 2015 to 2025, there have been 20 expiries. We've had seven that have renewed. We have three that have sold on the market separately. We've had seven that have taken up the backstop offers for the eligible leasees. Sorry, I didn't hear what the- Sorry.

So you very softly spoke about it. Sorry. That option that you were talking, what was that? What's the backstop option? The backstop option was available for leasees who have resided in their properties from the 30th of September 2020, and they'd been longstanding leasees. So they were invited to apply for the backstop offer, which essentially was, what it is, is if you chose not to or wished to have an option to sell your leasehold interest, that you could take up the backstop offer which the trust board offered on all lease, leasehold interests.

So what's that? That are owner-occupiers. That are owner-occupied. And those leasees, that wished to, took up that offer. That's the 180K, isn't it? Yes. Yeah. So the question really I was getting to is when that happens, those people take... So that's 20 expiries.

That counts for 17 of them. Let's leave it at that point. But what happens with those properties and what's the- Yes. What does the trust do? Yes. What does your master plan- Sure, yeah... what does your master plan say you're allowed to do? Yes. If anything. Okay. So- And what's the revenue stream you get from those properties vis-à-vis what you're getting from leasing them?

The, in the terms of the lease, which were put in place in the 1920s, there are provisions with respect to if a person chooses not to renewBecause it is their choice. If they choose not to renew, then there is an auction process, which we have tried to modernize, but it was written in 1920, and if the auction is unsuccessful, the property reverts to the Trust Board.

We then basically ensure the home is in reasonable order. Often there's some refurbishment needed, and we make them available as homes for Aucklanders on a rental basis. And the revenue stream from the rental versus lease? It depends on the particular property.

It doesn't have as much operating cash flow on the day one as a residential rental versus ground lease. But over the time of a 21-year period, which is the term of a perpetual lease, residential rental actually is a reasonable return. So you have to have a very long-term view on these things.

Okay. We have gone a bit over, but I do have a question from Tom, and I'd like to take it. Yep. And then we'll finish there. Sure. Thank you. So property comes on the market. I go to view the property. Uh-huh. Am I told at that time that the property is on a leasehold arrangement? Yes. The real estate agents in Auckland are very well used to leasehold. There's a lot of leasehold in Auckland, and also the Trust Board, before it will sign the reassignment of the lease requires that purchaser of the lease, the interest, has received legal advice as to what the

obligations and pluses and minuses of leasehold are. So as a prospective buyer, I would know that this is the element of purchasing the property that I am taking into my consideration. I could purchase a freehold property further down the road. Yes. I could purchase this one on the leasehold land.

Yes. And that is the decision that I make as the buyer. Yes. Okay. Thank you. Thank you. Sophon, just very quick. Go ahead. Do the new purchasers know how much leasehold payment they need to actually... Yes. Yes. On their purchase. Mm. Right.

The ground lease has been set. It was set 21 years prior or 10 years prior. Yeah. So they know that the next lot of leases are coming up at a specific time. And they know exactly when the next renew is. Right. Well, thank you very much. Thank you. It's been very good. I was in Cornwall Park in the weekend, and it is an absolutely beautiful place. Thank you.
Thank you. Thank you much also for the work that you do- Mm. To help maintain such an amazing space. Thank you. Thank you very much. Sure. If, Chair, for any further
